title: Lots of lots of German related topics and a German Why
display_title: Lots of lots of German related topics and a German Why
wikitext:
[]: Lots of lots of German related topics (and a German). Why?             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Sun Oct 28, 2007 3:37 pm I have always wondered why there are so many German words. For now I have been assuming the following: 1: Giving us the nudge of psychological meaning behind the series i.e. Freud, Nietzche, (who were German just to sound obvious)etc. 2: Giving us the nudge of the sereis being related to some German piece of work i.e. Der Rings des Nibelungen (Wagner), especially musical pieces of Beethoven, Handel, and Bach appearing seems to strengthen this point (they were all German, but Handel worked in England) 3: Or maybe Anno was fascinated with Germany (maybe he like the Bavarian Lederhosen).

[]:             Quote Post by BobBQ » Sun Oct 28, 2007 3:44 pm Japan and Germany have a long history of being buddy-buddy, as I understand. NGE is by no means the only animanga I've seen with large numbers of German cultural references.

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Sun Oct 28, 2007 3:49 pm Interesting. Germany being friends with Japan? Umm...only during the Meiji ishin (a bit of help with the Imperial constitution) and the second world war...oh, and being a bit of economical partner recently, but that was just recently...still intrigued.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sun Oct 28, 2007 3:49 pm Smith: Their buddy-buddy-ness didn't just have to do with WW2, did it?

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Sun Oct 28, 2007 4:00 pm If it was, then it would not have been a very GOOD partnership...I hope it was not just the WW2 thing.

[]:             Quote Post by CitizenGeek » Sun Oct 28, 2007 4:24 pm I've really thought about why NGE has such a connection with Germany. It's probably got a lot to do with what you listed, The Imperialist. Maybe Anno just thought German sounded cool? :p

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Sun Oct 28, 2007 4:26 pm I also like German... that's why I do german. Heee heee, Anno in Lederhosen!

[]:             Quote Post by CitizenGeek » Sun Oct 28, 2007 5:04 pm The Imperialist wrote: I also like German... that's why I do german. Heee heee, Anno in Lederhosen!

[]:             Quote Post by BobBQ » Sun Oct 28, 2007 5:05 pm Sailor Star Dust wrote: Smith: Their buddy-buddy-ness didn't just have to do with WW2, did it?

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sun Oct 28, 2007 6:40 pm For further reading look up the Siebold Society, founded by Philipp Franz von Siebold (1796-1866) and chaired at one time by Dr. Constantin von Brandenstein-Zeppelin (a plausible candidate to be Asuka's grandfather).

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Sun Oct 28, 2007 8:35 pm Hmm, Germany and Japan have long been percieved to be extremely formal, rigid cultures with a very strict adherence to protocol. Is it a case of the Japanese modelling themselves after Prussian discipline and militarism, or is it that their own values matched that of the Germans who helped them? I think it's the latter. On a side note, Germans appear to have the coolest technical terminology I've seen.

[]:             Quote Post by BobBQ » Sun Oct 28, 2007 10:29 pm UrsusArctos wrote: On a side note, Germans appear to have the coolest technical terminology I've seen.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Mon Oct 29, 2007 11:26 am by the same logic "Bleach" is obsessed with Spanish

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Tue Oct 30, 2007 5:06 pm Hmm. Interesting.

[]:             Quote Post by Twsa'teotu » Wed Feb 18, 2009 3:13 pm Mr. Tines wrote: For further reading look up the Siebold Society, founded by Philipp Franz von Siebold (1796-1866) and chaired at one time by Dr. Constantin von Brandenstein-Zeppelin (a plausible candidate to be Asuka's grandfather).

[]:             Quote Post by Holy Diver » Wed Feb 18, 2009 3:26 pm Germany and Japan are like jelly and peanut butter. 1. Both prize martial skill and have strong military traditions(especially the Prussians). 2. They fought a war together. The bonds forged by war are hard to break. 3. Their cultures share several key values. 4. Germany helped bring Japan into the modern era. 5. They both have auto industries that are helping to crush American car makers (though stupid business policies have done more than their fair share of damage).

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Feb 18, 2009 3:42 pm Age, family name, connections with Japan (through the Siebold Society, which is a long-standing German/Japan cultural exchange organization -- there is a Siebold University in Nagasaki, for example). The version on this parallel married into German nobility some time after Kyoko's birth date; no red-headed part-Japanese grand-child here. In a world where there was an esoteric side to the link between recent allies, then things could have gone differently.

[]:             Quote Post by Twsa'teotu » Wed Feb 18, 2009 7:14 pm >Tines - is this from a supplement or something?

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Feb 18, 2009 7:18 pm IIRC this chap was first identified as a candidate for Asuka's Zeppelin ancestor in a thread on EvaMade.

[]:             Quote Post by Twsa'teotu » Wed Feb 18, 2009 7:20 pm I'm really confused about what your rationale for this is; what was the reason?
summary: []: Lots of lots of German related topics (and a German). Why?             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Sun Oct 28, 2007 3:37 pm I have always wondered why there are so many German words. For now I have been assuming the following: 1: Giving us the nudge of psychological meaning behind the series i.e. Freud, Nietzche, (who were German just to sound obvious)etc. 2: Giving us the nudge of the sereis being related to some German pie
